summary: McKenzie walks through the technical process of running a WordPress blog as a subdirectory of a Rails application using Nginx as a reverse proxy. He argues that despite Rails being the main app framework, WordPress is the superior blogging platform and worth integrating. The key architectural decision is placing the blog on a subdirectory rather than a subdomain for SEO benefits. The setup involves creating a WordPress theme from the Rails app's design, installing WordPress on a private URL, and configuring Nginx to proxy requests.
key_points: Blogs should live on subdirectories rather than subdomains for SEO reasons, even when the main app runs on a different stack
WordPress is vastly superior to Rails blogging platforms, making cross-stack integration worthwhile
ThemesPress can generate a WordPress theme from existing HTML/CSS for $10, saving significant development time
Nginx reverse proxy lets WordPress appear to be part of the Rails app while running independently on a private server
money_quotes: Since Bingo Card Creator is powered by Rails, ideally I'd be using a Rails blog platform, but frankly, they're all vastly inferior to WordPress.
I could have slapped up a WordPress blog on a subdomain and called it a day, but I always advise people to put blogs on subdirectories for SEO reasons, so I thought I'd do it properly.
If you're not a PHP-speaking web designer, this step can take a lot of time. Or, alternatively, you can just pay $10 and use ThemesPress.
This is well worth your time.
It is easy to bork this setup if you don't understand WordPress and Nginx configuration files.
key_insight: Pragmatic integration of the best tool for each job — WordPress for blogging, Rails for the app — beats ideological purity about using a single stack, especially when SEO demands subdirectory placement.
